pullRequest.title: DOM-45299 add endpoint for UI to call in order to implement hide project copy button

nodes.body: Moved billing tag to happen before any thing is created, so if it fails, we don't end up with orphaned resources in the git pod or the remote git provider

toying around with the new "best practice" for apis: https://docs.google.com/document/d/18UQ242kR5Cb-_RaSElnmIQui4pu6seh7YqPlxWG5snA/edit#heading=h.nscqknnl8lti. Should I make another routes file that doesn't include the `/v4/projects` at the beginning of the url?

_Apologies for the wall of text..._ 🐱 

The UI is already using `v4/projects/permissions/all` to get the `canCopy` and `canFork` attributes. Should we integrate the two solutions? Would this new logic apply to fork operation too? I imagine one possible option is to have a new subresource of project `v4/projects/:projectId/permissions` and have all the logic of "canCopy" in one place. Maybe `permissions` doesn't make sense for the current logic, so something more generic could work better, like `v4/projects/:projectId/actions` and then have a list of actions 
```
[{id: "copy", supported: true, reason: null, uri: "http://..../v4/projects/:projectId/copy"},
 {id: "fork", supported: false, reason: "...", uri: "..."}]
``` 
I think some approach like this would give a more "RESTful" navigation to the API too (collection/id/collection).

About the versions, I would avoid mixing v1 and v4 in the same path.
`v4/projects` is the internal, legacy, "non-standard" version of the API. Then we have `projects/v1/projects` which is the "public", closer to the standard one. We could start a new API even closer to the standard using `/{domain}/v1/projects/:projectId/actions` format (only defining that single endpoint for now). The {domain} could be "develop" as a more coarse grained product domain, encapsulating projects, environments, workspaces, etc. IMO for this particular ticket, we should be fine with just defining a new endpoint under the legacy v4, or creating a brand new API `/develop/v1` with only this new endpoint.

If we do a new API, I would go with the spec first approach (like public API) rather than resty. It's easy to make mistakes in resty definitions, that go unseen in code reviews and are only detected when we do a system test. The spec first approach means we manually define the yaml we are going to give to customers, so the code review is easier (not having to guess what the auto-generated swagger is going to look like from resty files and scala code). And the implementation must use the generated code, so there is an extra layer of validation that the spec is correct.
We could make it a class, so it's mockable in unit tests

nit: From this [scala style guide](https://docs.scala-lang.org/style/files.html), I think we should put this and the class below in its own file (called RepoManException.scala). I think it makes it easier to find, helps keep files smaller too.
nit: maybe "commons" or "utils" instead of "lib"? To be a bit more specific on the library intent.
renamed to utils

I agree tthat it's easier to understand and work with dtos that are defined in their own files/logical groupings. The  projects/dto.scala file is really big. Maybe we should file a ticket to refactor everything out of the dto file.
I feel like this means that we should refactor the copyProject authorization
should add a deprecation annotation here
should add a deprecation annotation to this service method
Going to leave this as an object, because I think there is a scala test helper which you can use to mock objects `mockObject`

I couldn't find any example of that in our code. Do you know how it manages to mock objects? AFAIK objects are like static methods in Java, so to mock them you need to tamper with bytecode, it's not a "legal" override of the real implementation. If we never did this before, I don't think we should introduce it to the code. But if we are not introducing that in this PR, I guess we'll just refactor this to a class when we need to mock it somewhere.
TODO should probably move this
Is there a plan to add more supported actions to the API or is it mainly for copy/fork status?

I have no plans to add more things. However, whenever we work on features that require UI, sometimes there is a requirement for the UI to know if the user can do a certain action. When we have a requirement like that, we would extend this endpoint and tell the frontend ppl to use it.
nit: I think `v1` is the version of the "develop domain API". Maybe should be in the `developdomain.routes` file? And so projects routes defined in this file will all belong to the same API version.

'“API version” signifies the version of the API being accessed for that Domain'

TBH, I think the documentation is not specific enough. It doesn't explicitly say that the domain is what's versioned. I think it's up to us how we want to organize the endpoints.
updated the route file to include the version in the name of the routes file and addded it to the tag in the swagger.
Yes, I think you are right, it's the version of the APIs, not of the domain, sorry for the confusion.
Can we create a ticket to remove usages of the deprecated functions? Maybe we can have a ticket scheduled for 5.12+?
yes
https://dominodatalab.atlassian.net/browse/DOM-55614
updated to include issue key
I'm thinking if nucleus could be competing with repoman in this rate limit

nit: Have you considered the alternative of returning a list of actions instead of an object?
It's more RESTful in the sense that `supported-actions` becomes a collection of supported actions. `copyProject` and `forkProject` boolean filters become an action type filter maybe? like `type=COPY` or `type=FORK` or no filter returns both. The implementation of such filter can be using an enum or sub-typing `SupportedActions`, not sure what's better, I would try the latter first.

I couldn't find examples of REST where an endpoint gives a single object, except for the `/collection/:id` cases. I'm not sure if it has some disadvantage to return an object under `/supported-actions` either, other than not following the "REST practice". Maybe extensibility? like the `/collection/:id` approach can be intuitively extended to other sub-resources of the actions if needed, but if `/supported-actions` gives an object, extending to sub-resources of the individual actions may be less natural. But these actions are unlikely to ever need sub-resources anyways.

BTW, I comment this here because this is going directly to the API. There is also the more boilerplate alternative of keeping whatever is useful in the code and then have a mapper in the API "presentation" layer (controller) to format it to the RESTful API dto that we want.

I like the mapping of action name to action better because it's more explicit and more convenient on the client side. The client consumer doesn't have to iterate through the results to get what they want, or combine them all into a map

Maybe we could think of `supported-actions` as a collection, but the response is just formatted as a map, not a list.
I updated the query parameters to behave more like generic filtering parameters

The standard should be more strict in the definition of a collection, so that all APIs following it would return the same structure. The idea is that we return a collection of items in a certain format. Typically that is an array wrapped in some pagination details. Like
```
{totalCount: 10, paginationDetails: {...}, items: [ <the array of items> ] }
```
For the standard to be effective it should enforce this format, so that all Domino APIs will have the same format. If each team defines its own collection format, it will be a headache for consumers and it will not be standarized. Even when no pagination is needed, it should probably honor the format and just return null in the pagination details.

This is unfortunately implemented randomly throughout the code base. `"api-server-utils/src/main/scala/domino/apiserverutils/pagination/PaginationFilter.scala` seems to have been intended to be the BP. It is however missing things like totalCount

nit: Have you considered putting all this logic inside a private method? something like
`case Some(mainRepo) => getGitBasedProjectCopySupport(mainRepo)`

I think in order for the rate limit to work, we need `github` to be injected in a singleton scope (so all threads will share the same github API client instance). I guess it's thread safe, otherwise the rate limit config wouldn't make sense.

It looks like the github rate limits that we will be subject to is the "Primary rate limit for authenticated users": https://docs.github.com/en/rest/using-the-rest-api/rate-limits-for-the-rest-api?apiVersion=2022-11-28#primary-rate-limit-for-authenticated-users

and then secondary rate limit, which is about too many requests to individual endpoints.

I think we should be good if we add a singleton annotation here. We can't make repoman and nucleus-frontend us the same client because they are on separate pods.
nit: Maybe these values could be the defaults. So "return all" or "no filter" would be the default operation, unless otherwise specified.
Great unit tests!
